title: Diabetic Foot Infection (DFI)
objectives: Classify diabetic foot infections by severity
Understand the microbiology and antibiotic selection
Recognize osteomyelitis and when to involve surgery
Apply the IDSA/IWGDF guidelines for DFI management

sections.title: Overview and Risk Factors
sections.content:
Diabetic foot infections (DFI) are a common and serious complication of diabetes, often leading to hospitalization and amputation.

**Epidemiology:**
• 15-25% of diabetics will develop a foot ulcer in their lifetime
• 40-80% of foot ulcers become infected
• DFI precedes 85% of lower extremity amputations in diabetes
• 5-year mortality after amputation: ~50%

**Risk Factors for Infection:**
• Peripheral neuropathy (loss of protective sensation)
• Peripheral arterial disease (poor perfusion)
• Prior foot ulcer or amputation
• Structural deformity (Charcot foot, hammertoes)
• Poor glycemic control
• Long duration of diabetes
• Immunosuppression

sections.title: Classification: IDSA/IWGDF Severity
sections.content:
**Uninfected:**
• Ulcer present but no signs of infection
• No purulence or inflammation

**Mild (PEDIS Grade 2):**
• Local infection only
• Erythema >0.5 cm but ≤2 cm around ulcer
• Purulent discharge, warmth, tenderness, induration
• No systemic signs
• Superficial (skin/subcutaneous only)

**Moderate (PEDIS Grade 3):**
• Local infection with:
  - Erythema >2 cm, OR
  - Involvement of deeper structures (fascia, muscle, tendon, bone), OR
  - Abscess, gangrene, or necrotizing infection
• Metabolically stable (no SIRS)

**Severe (PEDIS Grade 4):**
• Any foot infection PLUS systemic inflammatory response
• SIRS criteria: ≥2 of:
  - Temp >38°C or <36°C
  - HR >90
  - RR >20 or PaCO2 <32
  - WBC >12K or <4K or >10% bands
• May have DKA, hypotension, altered mental status

sections.title: Microbiology
sections.content:
**Mild, Acute Infections:**
• Usually monomicrobial
• Gram-positive cocci predominate
• Staphylococcus aureus (most common overall)
• Beta-hemolytic streptococci (Groups A, B, C, G)

**Moderate-Severe, Chronic, or Previously Treated:**
• Often polymicrobial
• S. aureus, Streptococci (still present)
• Gram-negatives: E. coli, Proteus, Klebsiella, Pseudomonas
• Anaerobes: Bacteroides, Peptostreptococcus

**MRSA Considerations:**
• Risk factors: prior MRSA, recent hospitalization, prior antibiotics, high local prevalence
• Cover empirically if risk factors present

**Pseudomonas:**
• Consider if: wound soaking, tropical climate, failure of prior antibiotics
• NOT routinely covered in all DFI

sections.title: Diagnosis and Workup
sections.content:
**Clinical Diagnosis:**
• DFI is diagnosed CLINICALLY (presence of ≥2 inflammatory signs)
• Do NOT culture uninfected wounds

**When Infection Present:**

**Wound Culture:**
• Deep tissue or bone is preferred
• Swab cultures less reliable but acceptable if deep tissue not feasible
• Cleanse wound before culturing
• Culture before antibiotics if possible

**Labs:**
• CBC (WBC, differential)
• CMP (glucose, renal function)
• CRP and ESR (baseline, follow for response)
• HbA1c if not recent
• Blood cultures if severe or febrile

**Imaging:**
• X-ray: First-line for all moderate-severe DFI
  - Look for: soft tissue gas, foreign body, osteomyelitis
• MRI: Best for osteomyelitis detection (sensitivity ~90%)
• CT: Alternative if MRI contraindicated

**Probe-to-Bone Test:**
• Positive if sterile probe touches bone through ulcer
• PPV ~90% if ulcer >2 cm² with visible bone

sections.title: Osteomyelitis Recognition
sections.content:
**When to Suspect Osteomyelitis:**
• Ulcer present >6 weeks
• Ulcer overlying bony prominence
• Positive probe-to-bone test
• Sausage toe appearance
• ESR >70 mm/hr
• Exposed bone or visible bone in wound

**Imaging:**
• X-ray: Insensitive early (changes take 2-3 weeks)
  - Findings: periosteal reaction, cortical destruction, focal osteopenia
• MRI: Test of choice
  - Low T1 signal + high T2/STIR signal in bone marrow
  - Sensitivity 90%, specificity 80%

**Bone Biopsy:**
• Gold standard for diagnosis AND microbiology
• Obtain if: diagnosis uncertain, failed empiric therapy, planning surgery
• Culture + histopathology

**Treatment Implications:**
• Osteomyelitis requires prolonged antibiotics (6 weeks IV or PO) AND often surgical debridement

sections.title: Antibiotic Selection
sections.content:
**MILD DFI (Outpatient):**
• Target: Gram-positives (Staph, Strep)
• Duration: 1-2 weeks
• Options:
  - Cephalexin 500mg QID, OR
  - Amoxicillin-clavulanate 875/125mg BID, OR
  - Clindamycin 300mg TID (if penicillin allergy)
• Add MRSA coverage if risk factors: TMP-SMX or doxycycline

**MODERATE DFI:**
• Broader spectrum, may need IV initially
• Target: Gram-positives + gram-negatives ± anaerobes
• Options:
  - Ampicillin-sulbactam 3g IV q6h, OR
  - Piperacillin-tazobactam 3.375g IV q6h, OR
  - Ertapenem 1g IV q24h
• Duration: 2-4 weeks (longer if osteomyelitis)

**SEVERE DFI:**
• Broad spectrum, IV therapy, often surgical emergency
• Options:
  - Vancomycin + piperacillin-tazobactam, OR
  - Vancomycin + meropenem (if Pseudomonas or ESBL risk)
• Tailor based on cultures

sections.title: Surgical and Adjunctive Management
sections.content:
**Surgical Consultation Indications:**
• Abscess requiring drainage
• Necrotizing fasciitis or gas gangrene
• Deep space infection
• Osteomyelitis requiring debridement/amputation
• Non-healing wounds needing debridement
• Vascular assessment for revascularization

**When Amputation May Be Needed:**
• Extensive tissue loss
• Unreconstructable vascular disease
• Severe/refractory osteomyelitis
• Life-threatening sepsis from limb source

**Wound Care:**
• Debridement of necrotic tissue
• Offloading (total contact cast, surgical shoe)
• Moist wound healing environment
• Compression if no significant PAD

**Vascular Assessment:**
• ABI (ankle-brachial index)
• Toe pressures
• Revascularization may be needed before wound will heal

sections.title: Disposition and Follow-up
sections.content:
**Outpatient (Mild DFI):**
• Reliable patient
• No systemic signs
• Adequate wound care resources
• Close follow-up (48-72 hours)

**Admit (Moderate-Severe DFI):**
• Systemic signs (fever, SIRS, DKA)
• Need for IV antibiotics
• Surgical intervention needed
• Social factors preventing outpatient management
• Severe PAD requiring urgent vascular evaluation

**Follow-up:**
• Re-evaluate wound at 48-72 hours
• Adjust antibiotics based on culture results
• Monitor inflammatory markers (CRP, ESR)
• Address glycemic control
• Multidisciplinary: ID, surgery, vascular, endocrine, podiatry

**Prevention:**
• Daily foot inspection
• Proper footwear
• Regular podiatry care
• Good glycemic control
• Smoking cessation

keyTakeaways: DFI severity: Mild (local <2cm), Moderate (deep or >2cm), Severe (SIRS present)
Mild acute DFI: Gram-positives (Staph/Strep); Chronic/severe: polymicrobial
Probe-to-bone positive? Suspect osteomyelitis – get MRI
Osteomyelitis requires 6 weeks of antibiotics ± surgical debridement
Surgical consultation for: abscess, necrotizing infection, osteomyelitis, non-healing wounds

references.citation: Lipsky BA, Berendt AR, Deery HG, et al. IDSA Clinical Practice Guideline for the Diagnosis and Treatment of Diabetic Foot Infections. Clin Infect Dis. 2012;54(12):e132-e173
references.keyContent: Definitive IDSA guideline with PEDIS classification and antibiotic selection

references.citation: Lipsky BA, Senneville E, Abbas ZG, et al. IWGDF/IDSA Guidelines on the Diagnosis and Treatment of Diabetes-related Foot Infections. Clin Infect Dis. 2024;78(Suppl 1):S3-S24
Schaper NC, van Netten JJ, Apelqvist J, et al. Practical Guidelines on the prevention and management of diabetic foot disease (IWGDF 2019 update). Diabetes Metab Res Rev. 2020;36 Suppl 1:e3266
references.keyContent: International Working Group guidelines freely available

references.citation: Newman LG, Waller J, Palestro CJ, et al. Unsuspected osteomyelitis in diabetic foot ulcers. JAMA. 1991;266(9):1246-1251

reviewQuestions.question: A 58-year-old diabetic presents with a foot ulcer. There is 2.5 cm of surrounding erythema, purulent drainage, and the probe touches bone. Vitals are normal. What is the severity classification?
reviewQuestions.explanation: Erythema >2 cm OR deeper structure involvement (probe-to-bone positive suggests osteomyelitis) classifies this as moderate DFI. It would be severe if SIRS criteria were present.

reviewQuestions.question: Which imaging modality is most sensitive for detecting diabetic foot osteomyelitis?
reviewQuestions.explanation: MRI is the most sensitive (~90%) and specific (~80%) imaging modality for osteomyelitis. X-ray changes lag 2-3 weeks behind infection and are less sensitive early.

reviewQuestions.question: A patient with mild DFI and no prior antibiotics or MRSA risk factors should receive empiric coverage for which organisms?
reviewQuestions.explanation: Mild, acute DFI without risk factors for resistant organisms is typically caused by gram-positive cocci (S. aureus, beta-hemolytic streptococci). Cephalexin or amoxicillin-clavulanate are appropriate.

mcqs.stem: Which of the following is the MOST important initial step in evaluating a patient with suspected diabetic foot infection?

options.text: Obtain a thorough history and physical examination
options.explanation: A comprehensive history and physical examination is the foundation of clinical reasoning in diabetic foot infection. It guides all subsequent diagnostic and management decisions.
Advanced imaging may be indicated but should be guided by clinical findings, not ordered reflexively.
Treatment should generally follow an appropriate diagnostic evaluation unless the patient is critically unstable.

options.text: Consult a specialist before any workup
options.explanation: Initial evaluation should be performed by the treating physician; specialist consultation follows based on findings.

mcqs.stem: A patient presents with classic features of diabetic foot infection. Which clinical reasoning principle is MOST important in avoiding diagnostic error?

options.text: Search satisficing - stop looking after the first diagnosis
options.explanation: Premature closure (stopping the diagnostic process too early) is a common cognitive error that leads to missed diagnoses.
Systematic consideration of differential diagnoses, including must-not-miss conditions, is essential for avoiding premature closure and anchoring bias.
While pattern recognition (System 1 thinking) is useful, it should be balanced with analytical reasoning (System 2) to avoid cognitive biases.

options.text: Order every available test to rule out all possibilities
options.explanation: Indiscriminate testing leads to false positives, unnecessary procedures, and increased cost without improving diagnostic accuracy.

mcqs.stem: When managing diabetic foot infection, which approach best supports evidence-based practice?

options.text: Follow institutional protocols regardless of patient factors
options.explanation: Evidence-based practice integrates best evidence with clinical expertise AND patient values/circumstances.

options.text: Base decisions solely on clinical experience
options.explanation: Clinical experience is valuable but can introduce bias; it should be combined with current evidence.

options.text: Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values
options.explanation: Evidence-based medicine is the conscientious integration of best research evidence, clinical expertise, and patient values in making clinical decisions.

options.text: Always defer to the most recent published study
options.explanation: Individual studies should be appraised for quality and relevance; systematic reviews and guidelines generally provide stronger evidence.
